Shyamji Krishna Varma

[Azaadi](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/azaadi/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Shyamji Krishna Varma

### Founder of India House

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Oct 04, 2023

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

4

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!4NfY!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe09527cf-883f-46b3-943c-f8d5e630656a_220x239.webp)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!4NfY!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe09527cf-883f-46b3-943c-f8d5e630656a_220x239.webp)

He was the mentor to many leading lights of India’s revolutionary movement, ranging from Veer Savarkar to Lala Hardayal to Madame Bhikaji Cama. He gave up a flourishing legal career to take part in the freedom struggle. He founded the India House in London, which would become the center for most Indian revolutionaries, operating outside of India or in exile.

Shyamji Krishna Varma, one of the most brilliant minds of the modern era, a selfless patriot, who gave his everything for the nation’s freedom. His admiration for Swami Dayanand Saraswati’s cultural nationalism and Herbert Spencer’s philosophy of resistance shaped his radical worldview. Spencer’s dictum, *“Resistance to aggression is not simply justified, but imperative,”* became a guiding principle for Varma’s activism

He was born in the year of India’s great mutiny, 1857 on October 4, in Mandvi, Kutch to Krushnadas Bhanushali, a labourer in a cotton press and Gomatibai. He belonged to the Bhanushali community who are of Kshatriya origin and are settled mostly in Kutch and speak Kutchi. His ancestors were from Bhachunda, a village now located in Abdasa taluk of Kutch, having lost his mother at age of 11 he was raised by his grandmother.

His journey from Mandvi to Mumbai, from a cotton press laborer’s son to a Sanskrit scholar honored in Kashi, is nothing short of remarkable. The fact that he was the *first non-Brahmin* to be conferred the title of Pandit in 1877 speaks volumes about both his erudition and the quiet defiance he embodied against caste orthodoxy.

His meeting with Swami Dayananda Saraswati in 1875 was clearly a turning point. The Arya Samaj’s emphasis on Vedic revivalism and national pride must have resonated deeply with a young man already steeped in Sanskrit and seeking a higher purpose. That spiritual foundation later fused with political radicalism, giving rise to a thinker who could challenge empire with both pen and principle.

Shyamji Krishna Varma’s time at Balliol College, Oxford, beginning in 1879, was marked by academic brilliance. His lecture on *“The Origin of Writing in India”* at the Royal Asiatic Society was so well received that he was elected a non-resident member—a rare honor for an Indian at the time. This moment symbolized not just scholarly recognition, but also his early belief in dialogue and intellectual diplomacy.

But the return to India in 1885 marked a shift. His legal practice in Ajmer and investments in cotton presses gave him financial independence—crucial for a man who would later fund revolutionaries. His appointments as Diwan in Ratlam and Udaipur were prestigious, yet it was his tenure in Junagadh that proved pivotal. A humiliating encounter with a British agent there in 1897 shattered his remaining illusions about the fairness of British rule. That resignation wasn’t just from a post—it was from the idea that reform within the system was possible.

His disillusionment with the Congress Moderates and alignment with Tilak’s assertive nationalism marked a decisive shift—from reformist hopes to revolutionary resolve.

His association with Bal Gangadhar Tilak wasn’t just political—it was deeply philosophical. Tilak’s fiery stance during the Age of Consent Bill controversy and his emphasis on *Swaraj as a birthright* resonated with Varma’s own growing impatience with British paternalism. Their correspondence reveals mutual admiration; Tilak even praised Varma’s “self-sacrificing spirit” in a letter, acknowledging how the freer atmosphere of England allowed Varma to do what was impossible in India at the time.

Settling at the Inner Temple, Varma immersed himself in the writings of Herbert Spencer, whose dictum—*“Resistance to aggression is not simply justified, but imperative”*—became a cornerstone of Varma’s political philosophy. Spencer’s evolutionary sociology offered Varma a framework to critique imperialism not just as political domination, but as a systemic moral failure/

He bought an expensive home at Highgate, where he would set up the India House on July 1, 1905, which was meant for Indian students in London to accommodate them.

The house at 65 Cromwell Avenue, Highgate, became far more than a residenc, it was a crucible of revolution. Shyamji Krishna Varma envisioned it as a sanctuary for Indian students, but also as an incubator for nationalist thought. With Henry Hyndman, the socialist and staunch supporter of Indian self-rule, presiding over the inauguration, and luminaries like Lala Lajpat Rai, Madam Bhikaji Cama, and Dadabhai Naoroji in attendance, the event was a symbolic declaration of ideological defiance.

India House quickly evolved into a nerve center for revolutionary activity. It hosted fiery debates, published *The Indian Sociologist*, and nurtured minds like Veer Savarkar, Madan Lal Dhingra, and Lala Har Dayal, each of whom would go on to leave an indelible mark on India’s freedom struggle.

In December 1904, on the first death anniversary of Herbert Spencer, Varma announced the *Herbert Spencer Indian Fellowships*, each worth ₹2,000, to support Indian graduates pursuing higher education in England. These fellowships weren’t merely academi,they came with a condition: recipients were not to accept any post under the British government upon their return. This clause was revolutionary in itself, subtly urging non-cooperation long before it became a mass movement.

He mirrored this initiative with fellowships in the name of Swami Dayananda Saraswati, honouring the Vedic reformer who had first ignited his nationalist spirit. Over time, Varma expanded the scheme to include fellowships named after Indian heroes like Rana Pratap, Bahadur Shah Zafar, Tipu Sultan, and Shivaji, as well as international revolutionaries from Egypt and Ireland.

These fellowships helped shape a generation of radical thinkers—Madan Lal Dhingra, Khudiram Bose, and others—who would carry the torch of resistance across continents. It’s remarkable how Varma used his financial independence not for comfort, but to build an ideological army. *The Indian Sociologist* and the India Home Rule Society were twin pillars of Shyamji Krishna Varma’s ideological offensive—one wielding the pen, the other organizing the people.

Launched in 1905, *The Indian Sociologist* was subtitled *“An Organ of Freedom, and Political, Social, and Religious Reform.”* It wasn’t just a magazine—it was a manifesto. Through its pages, Varma challenged the moral legitimacy of British rule, drawing heavily from Herbert Spencer’s philosophy. The journal famously quoted Spencer: *“Resistance to aggression is not simply justifiable but imperative.”* It became so influential that the British government banned its circulation in India by September 1907.

Just a month earlier, on February 18, 1905, Varma had founded the India Home Rule Society (IHRS) in London. Its mission was clear: *“to secure Home Rule for India, and to carry on a genuine Indian propaganda in this country by all practicable means.”* It was open only to Indians and quickly became a magnet for radical students and thinkers. The IHRS was modeled after Victorian public institutions but infused with revolutionary zeal.

Together, the journal and the society created a powerful echo chamber for nationalist thought—one that reached from the drawing rooms of London to the dusty streets of colonial India.

By the late 1900s, Shyamji Krishna Varma had become a thorn in the side of the British Empire, not through violence, but through the sheer force of his intellect and ideological defiance.

His writings in *The Indian Sociologist*—especially those advocating resistance to colonial aggression—were deemed seditious. On April 30, 1909, the Inner Temple disbarred him for “conduct unbecoming of a barrister,” citing his anti-British publications. It was a calculated move to delegitimize him professionally and politically. The British press vilified him, portraying him as a dangerous radical, and the government placed him under constant surveillance, monitoring his correspondence and movements.

But Varma was undeterred. Anticipating arrest, he relocated to Paris in 1907, continuing his revolutionary work from the continent. His exile didn’t silence him—it amplified his voice across Europe. And in a poetic twist of justice, over a century later in 2015, the Inner Temple posthumously reinstated his membership, acknowledging that he had not received a fair hearing and that his advocacy for Indian home rule was not incompatible with the values of the Bar.

Among the many Indian students who wished to avail his scholarship was Savarkar, who in his application, tressed upon his participation in the Swadeshi movement, the college debarring him and his relationship with Tilak and Paranjpe.

This was Tilak’s recommendation later

*“He is from Bombay, his name is Savarkar. He graduated last year, a dynamic young man who has been in the forefront of the Swadeshi movement. Because of which he has had to incur the displeasure of the college management. He does not wish to enter Government service and his character is excellent”.*

*“For me independence and liberty are the very pulse and breath of a nation. From my boyhood, dear Sir up to this moment, the only theme of which I dream about during night and muse during daytime is the loss of freedom of my country and regaining it. “*

Savarkar’s letter to Shyamji Krishna Varma was more than a formal request—it was a manifesto. He emphasized his expulsion from Fergusson College for organizing a bonfire of foreign goods during the Swadeshi movement, his close ties with Tilak and Paranjpe, and his unwavering commitment to India’s liberation. Varma, recognizing the fire in this young man, granted him the scholarship, enabling Savarkar to travel to London in 1906 and join India House—a decision that would ripple through history.

The Shivaji scholarship, granted in May 1906, was more than financial aid—it was a rite of passage. The agreement, signed in the presence of Daji Nagesh Apte and Krishnaji Prabhakar Khadilkar, with Tilak as the designated mediator, reflects the deep trust and ideological kinship among these revolutionaries. It’s fascinating how even their legal documents were infused with a sense of dharma and mutual accountability.

When Savarkar met Shyamji Krishna Varma, he took him around London, and told him this is your Kurukshetra now, where you have to fight your battle. True to his name, he was the Krishna to Savarkar’s Arjun, guiding him in the battlefield.

By 1907, India House had become a melting pot of radical thought, where Savarkar sharpened his ideological blade, Madam Cama stirred nationalist fervor, Lala Har Dayal explored anarchist philosophy, and Madan Lal Dhingra found the conviction that would lead to his fateful act. The presence of Irish republicans, Russian émigrés, and European socialists only deepened the intellectual ferment. It was a global salon of resistance, where revolution was not whispered—it was debated, printed, and plotted.

Shyamji Krishna Varma’s decision to relocate to Paris in 1907 was both strategic and prescient. With British surveillance tightening and *The Indian Sociologist* under scrutiny, he knew the net was closing. Entrusting India House to Savarkar was no small gesture—it was a passing of the torch. And in Paris, Varma continued his mission, now from the shadows, sustaining the movement through correspondence, publications, and alliances with continental radicals.

When the British government attempted to extradite him from France, they were met with stiff resistance—not just from Varma himself, but from influential French politicians who admired his intellect and respected his cause. France, with its own revolutionary legacy, became a sympathetic ground for Varma’s mission. His presence in Paris allowed him to build alliances across Europe, and his writings and speeches began to resonate even within progressive circles in Britain.

The arrest of Veer Savarkar in 1909, following the assassination of Curzon Wyllie by Madan Lal Dhingra, was a flashpoint. Shyamji Krishna Varma, ever the strategist, mobilized public opinion across Europe against Savarkar’s extradition and treatment. He used *The Indian Sociologist*, personal networks, and sympathetic European journalists to frame the arrest as a violation of civil liberties and national dignity.

His flight from Paris in 1914, prompted by the visit of King George V and the tightening Franco-British alliance, marked the beginning of his final exile. Geneva, though neutral, imposed a ban on political activity during the war, effectively silencing him. Under virtual house arrest, Varma’s only apparent ally was Dr. Briess, head of the so-called *Pro-India Committee*. The bitter irony? Briess was a British informant, feeding intelligence back to the very empire Varma had spent his life resisting.

Yet even in isolation, Varma’s vision didn’t dim. His offer of 10,000 francs to the League of Nations to endow a lectureship on *freedom and independence* was a bold attempt to institutionalize the ideals he had championed for decades. But British pressure ensured its rejection. His subsequent appeal to the Swiss government, though met with polite applause, was similarly stonewalled. It must have been a crushing blow—to be applauded, but not heard.

Shyamji Krishna Varma’s final years in Geneva were marked by solitude and quiet resistance. Though disillusioned by betrayal and hemmed in by surveillance, he never abandoned the cause. His death on March 30, 1930, was deliberately suppressed by the British authorities, but the flame he lit could not be extinguished. Even within the walls of Lahore Jail, Bhagat Singh and his comrades paid homage to the man who had mentored a generation of revolutionaries.

Perhaps the most poetic gesture of all was his deal with the Swiss government: to preserve his and his wife Bhanumati’s ashes until India achieved independence. And true to his vision, on August 22, 2003, the ashes were finally returned to India by then Gujarat Chief Minister Narendra Modi, fulfilling Varma’s last wish. Today, they rest at Kranti Teerth in Mandvi, a memorial that echoes with the ideals he lived and died for.

He was, not just a freedom fighter—but a philosopher of resistance, a mentor of revolution, and a seer of Swaraj.
